children.text:
Navigating
Ambiguity: Latino USA’s coverage of U.S. Occupation in Puerto Rico

Until 1898, Puerto Rico was under
Spanish rule for 400 years. Following the Spanish-American War, U.S. military
forces invaded the island, asserting control through economic, political, and
military means. In 1952, Puerto Rico became a U.S. commonwealth. While
island-born individuals are U.S. citizens, they lack voting representation in
Congress, and the territory remains without full sovereignty. The colonization
of Puerto Rico is a recurring topic in the public radio show

.
Two of these episodes extensively address the theme: Episode 275 and Episode
348. By analyzing these two episodes of the show, I argue that

transcends binary narratives by incorporating diverse voices and using radio as
a creative, journalistic space that embraces ambiguity.

Latino USA was created at the
University of Texas at Austin and emerged from the desire to amplify Latine
voices. Maria Martin, the show’s creator and founder, envisioned a platform
that could “portray the whole of the Latino experience” (160). Christopher
Chavez posits that the creation of the show marked a disruption amidst the more
conventional NPR programming, while still operating within its institutional
framework. The show is deeply embedded within the experience of the show’s
creators. The hosts of the program, Maria Hinojosa and Maria Martin, along with
other members of their staff, must balance their roles as journalists and
Latina women engaging with U.S. Imperialism. Drawing from Gloria Anzaldua’s
concept of a “Tolerance for Ambiguity” from

Latino
USA illustrates how conflicting perspectives can be generative. The show avoids
taking explicit political stances but invites audiences to consider diverse points
of view, thus avoiding monolithic expressions of the Latine diaspora. Within
the episodes, hosts and producers are mediators who structure content to reflect
layered identities and contradictions. Taking also from Michele Hilmes, who argues
radio episodes are texts, I examine how the hosts and producers incorporate
“themes of flexibility, blurring, and ambivalence in their understandings of
lands and identities, and projects them to a wide audience for entertainment
and learning” (Jimenez 5). Latino USA embraces conflict by electing to focus on
the issues Latinos face, rather thoroughly, turning complexity into insight. Using
the free and open-source tool, AVAnnotate, I have tagged the episodes according
to Speaker, Editorial Decisions, Affiliations, Topics, and Interview Makeup, as
Hilmes suggests, radio programs use “genre, technique, style, narrative
structures, representational conventions, and modes of address developed over
decades of practice” (421). Latino USA follows a similar journalistic habitus to
NPR, but as the speaker and affiliations tags might suggest, the episodes
include voices not usually heard (Jimenez 86). The creation of metadata vis-à-vis
tagging allows for an interpretive framework of the episodes.

This episode of Latino USA aired
on July 26, 1998. It is 29 minutes in length and examines the effects of U.S. occupation
in Puerto Rico for the last 100 years. There are three segments included in the
show. The first segment focuses on the Young Bill and covers congressional
debates over Puerto Rico’s political status. The second addresses the Puerto
Rican diaspora in Nationalist Movements within the United States. Finally, the final
segment includes an interview and conversation between political analyst Juan
Manuel Garcia Passalacqua and Benny Frankie Cerezo, a former member of the House
of Representatives of Puerto Rico. The organization of the episode, beginning
with the timely political content, before transitioning to experiences within
the diaspora, exemplifies stacking within a radio show as a form of mediation. The
decision to feature the documentary, “From San Juan to Humboldt Park,” after the
island’s current political status exemplifies the long-standing desire for independence
from within the diaspora. Interestingly, however, is the perspective of violent
acts of rebellion from Oscar Lopez and Alicia Rodriguez, who were members of
the Armed Forces of National Liberation (FALN). The two were convicted of seditious
conspiracy, however, the producer of the documentary ensures the speakers are multi-faceted,
beginning with their love for Puerto Rico—such as the moment they arrived on
the island for the first time: “It's almost like how...maybe this is too
far-fetched, but people know their mother. People know their mother by their
scent. There was a scent that Puerto Rico gave, that I identified with” (17:46-17:56).
Archival sound adds to the urgency of their actions, as unnamed reporters describe
the group setting off bombs in the middle of the afternoon, “when Wall Street
business people were eating a leisurely Friday lunch” (19:30-19:45). The
interviewees, still in prison at the time of the recording, uphold their
support for the island, believing it was the only way to ensure independence. The
documentary ends with their prison sentences and their families’ hopes that
their sentences will be commuted. The documentary excerpt and its inclusion in
the episode navigate ambiguity by including the voices of two people who were
tried and convicted for their crimes, while also allowing them to describe
their motivations. In the third segment of the episode, Hinojosa mediates
between the interviewees, Garcia-Passalacqua and Cerezo. At one point, Garcia-Passalacqua
had been a member of the Popular Democratic Party, which advocated for Puerto
Rico to remain a commonwealth of the United States with self-governance (Riggs).
By contrast, Cerezo had been a member of the New Progressive Party, which
advocated for statehood (Cataldo). By choosing these two interviewees, the men
offer different perspectives, while also building upon the answers of one another.
Although it is unclear to whom Garcia-Passalacqua is aligned at the time of the
episode, his energy is lively: “I assure you, Maria, that the people of Puerto
Rico will not vote in a plebiscite until our 15 political prisoners are back
home” (21:54-22:04). It is likely that the interview was recorded ahead of time,
so the mediation that occurs falls within the editing. However, Hinojosa also
serves as a mediator, as she asks them to expand upon their claims regarding
nationalism, for example. Additionally, Hinojosa invites the interviewees to
speak from their perspective, also allowing them to be multi-faceted, outside
of their official capacities. The episode closes with Garcia-Passalacqua’s
claim that “America’s folly in the Caribbean has become evident. Cuba and
Puerto Rico will survive. American imperialism will end” (27:30-27:46). The
claim ends the interview on an emotional note, as Garcia-Passalacqua seems
triumphant. Within this segment, the various perspectives do not necessarily come
from U.S. interests for Puerto Rico to remain a commonwealth, but from two men
whose perspectives on what the island should be as they currently live on the
island.

Episode 348 aired more than a year
later than the episode mentioned previously. The episode came on December 19,
1999, several weeks after the Clinton Administration announced it would be
leaving the Island of Vieques within five years. The decision followed the
death of Vieques native, David Sanes Rodriguez, who was killed after the U.S. Navy
dropped a 500-pound bomb during training. The first segment includes the
announcement and reaction from public officials and activists. The second segment
features Garcia-Passalacqua, once again, offering commentary on the decision
before transitioning to diaspora protests in New York City. Despite extensive
coverage of Vieques, the producers follow the theme of U.S. occupation with a
reporter story on El Chorillo neighborhood in Panama, thus reflecting U.S.
Military presence in Latin America more broadly. Like the previous episode, the
segments begin with current events before also including analysis and protest
coverage. In the first segment of the episode, the anchor package features
seven voices, two of whom represent the U.S. Navy. The others are activists,
the mayor of Vieques, and the island’s commissioner—the island’s only voice in
Congress. Journalist Myrna Connolly speaks Spanish to express her frustration
over the events. Manuela Santiago, the Vieques mayor, is featured most in the
package. Santiago’s presence represents an ambiguity that Maria Martin explores
through scriptwriting. She introduces Santiago as a “pro-military Republican”
who rejected the military’s offer to buy the island to continue training (8:37-8:46).
Santiago responds confidently, assuring listeners that the island is for her people,
and not for sale. Also within this recording, Martin and the producers must
navigate the conflicting perspectives from U.S. Military officials and Puerto
Ricans, despite most of the interviewees indicating they no longer want the U.S.
military on the island. The producers allow the voices to offer different
perspectives regarding their dissatisfaction with the Clinton Administration. Martin’s
language also seeks to hold the military accountable, calling Sanes Rodriguez’
death a tragedy, as the package opens with an unnamed protester shouting, “Enough
is Enough!” The different perspectives between Puerto Rico and the U.S.
Military are at odds, as one group is calling for their removal, while military
officials try to safeguard the island. Kenneth Bacon, for example, invites the
Puerto Ricans to see the island from their own occupational perspective, where “Puerto
Ricans have to look at that just as the Navy and Marines have looked at it”
(8:09). It is likely that the producers/writers included the most compelling
claims to keep interest from listeners, but the various sound bites also create
the opportunity to explore the issue from diverse perspectives, navigating the
contradictions by giving them voice, and with butted-sound bites, to respond
directly to one another.

Latino USA embraces ambiguity to
create accurate representations of the Latine diaspora, as there is no one true
representation of the Latino/a. Within the radio show, interviewees are invited
to share their different perspectives, despite ambiguous situations. By integrating
diverse voices, engaging with colonial histories, and layering content through
format and stacking, the show resists reductionist narratives of identity. Its bilingual,
accented, and political nature offers a template for critical media production
that acknowledges complexity without oversimplification. Through radio episodes,
Latino USA shows that when ambiguity is managed, can be a tool for storytelling
and increased representation.

Anzaldua,
Gloria. “La Conciencia de La Mestiza/Towards a New Consciousness.”
, 5th ed., Aunt Lute, San Francisco, CA, 2022, pp. 83–96.

Díaz
Martín, Esther, and Héctor Domínguez Ruvalcaba. “Radiophonic Feminisms : The Sounds and Voices of
Contemporary Latina Radio Hosts in the U.S. Southwest, 1990-2017 / by Esther
Díaz Martín.” [University of Texas], 2018. Print.

Cataldo,
Adam L. “S&P Downgrades Another Puerto Rico Credit.”
